title: Bhāskara's pracchanna-bauddha charge — Advaita as smuggled-in Buddhism
attacker_text: Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya (Bhāskara), esp. introduction to 1.1 and the polemical aside at BSB 1.4.21 against māyā-vāda; supplemented by the cosmological sections at BSB 2.1
target_position_summary: Śaṅkara's Advaita as Bhāskara perceives it: Brahman alone real, world as māyā/mithyā, jñāna alone as the path to mokṣa, vivarta-vāda over pariṇāma-vāda. Bhāskara's charge is that this configuration of doctrines amounts to a Vedāntic re-importation of the Mādhyamika śūnyavāda position under Vedāntic vocabulary.

arguments.name: argument-1: structural equivalence of mithyā-vāda and śūnya-vāda
premises: The Mādhyamika position (śūnya-vāda) is that phenomena lack svabhāva (own-being); they are pratītya-samutpanna and are, in their final analysis, śūnya.
The Advaitin position is that the world is mithyā: anirvacanīya, sublatable in brahma-jñāna, lacking the kind of being Brahman has.
Functionally, both positions deny that phenomena have own-being in any robust sense; both treat phenomena as cognitively-real-but-ultimately-evacuated.

The differences between śūnya and mithyā operate at the level of substrate-affirmation (Advaita affirms Brahman as substrate; Mādhyamika has no substrate-affirmation), but at the level of the world's own status, the two positions are structurally equivalent.
The Advaitin therefore arrives at the Buddhist conclusion about the world by a different route, while preserving Vedic vocabulary for the substrate.
arguments.inference: The Advaitin position is structurally pracchanna-bauddha — Buddhism wearing Vedānta's clothes.
arguments.conclusion: A Vedāntin committed to the realist construal of the Brahma-Sūtras' pariṇāma-vāda must reject Advaita as a deviation from Vedāntic realism toward Buddhist illusionism.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Śaṅkara's mithyā-doctrine and his vivarta-vāda. The argument lands hard if mithyā is read as functional-equivalent-of-śūnya.

Gauḍapāda's Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā (especially the Vaitathya-Prakaraṇa and Alāta-Śānti-Prakaraṇa) is even more directly engaged: the explicit ajāti-vāda and the world-as-dream analogies are arguably the most Mādhyamika-influenced moments in the Vedānta corpus, and Bhāskara's charge applies most cleanly here.

positions_left_intact.position: Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita is not engaged by this charge, since the world's reality is preserved by the body-soul model. Bhāskara's polemic and Rāmānuja's polemic operate at different but compatible angles.
Madhva's Tattvavāda likewise; Bhāskara's polemic against Advaita is not a polemic against realist non-Advaitic Vedānta.

later_responses.response_text: Bhāmatī ad BSB 2.1 — defends the mithyā-doctrine by stressing the substrate-affirmation Bhāskara concedes is absent in śūnyavāda; the structural equivalence Bhāskara alleges is, on Vāchaspati's view, broken precisely at the substrate-axis
later_responses.verdict: the reply concedes the surface similarity but denies the structural equivalence; the residual disagreement is over how much weight substrate-affirmation carries in distinguishing two ontologies of phenomena.

later_responses.response_text: Advaita-Siddhi and Vedānta-Kalpa-Latikā — explicitly refute the pracchanna-bauddha charge by detailed exhibition of the doctrinal differences (substrate, theory of error, soteriology, status of bhakti)
later_responses.verdict: Madhusūdana's reply is the canonical Advaita defense; the structural-equivalence claim is shown to require ignoring multiple load-bearing differences.

arguments.user_thesis_test: The charge is genuinely pressing if one reads mithyā as evacuating phenomena of own-being in the Mādhyamika sense. The user's framework explicitly forbids the mithyā = asat reduction, which weakens this version of Bhāskara's charge to the extent that mithyā is reread under the user's constraint. Even so, the residual question (whether mithyā-with-substrate-affirmation is meaningfully different from śūnya-without-substrate-affirmation) is substantive. Partial-touch: the charge engages something real but is sharpest on a reading the user's framework already rejects.

arguments.name: argument-2: vivarta-vāda is incompatible with the sūtras' pariṇāma-language
premises: The Brahma-Sūtras at multiple loci use language of real production (e.g., janmādyasya yataḥ — that from which the origination etc. of this proceeds), which is naturally read as pariṇāma-vāda (real transformation).
Vivarta-vāda (apparent transformation, with substrate unchanged) does violence to this natural reading; it requires the sūtras' productive-causation language to be reinterpreted as merely apparent.
The Chāndogya 6.1.4 dṛṣṭānta of clay-and-pot is the canonical pariṇāma-image; its natural reading is that the pot is a real modification of clay.
Śaṅkara's vivarta-reading of clay-and-pot (treating the pot as nāma-rūpa-only) is exegetically strained.
The realist reading of the sūtras is therefore textually superior to the vivarta-reading.
arguments.inference: Vivarta-vāda is a forced exegesis to defend a doctrinal position the sūtras themselves do not endorse.
arguments.conclusion: Bhāskara's pariṇāma-vāda is the natural reading; Śaṅkara's vivarta-vāda is the deviation.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Śaṅkara's vivarta-reading of BSB 2.1 and his nāma-rūpa-reading of the Chāndogya.

positions_left_intact.position: Rāmānuja's body-soul causation is neither vivarta nor pariṇāma in the strict sense; Bhāskara's argument does not engage the alternative.

later_responses.response_text: Bhāmatī defends the vivarta-reading by arguing that sūtra-language operates at the vyāvahārika level and is consistent with vivarta at the pāramārthika level
later_responses.verdict: the reply requires a level-distinction Bhāskara does not accept; the disagreement reduces to whether the two-level ontology is principled or ad-hoc.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive at the exegetical level. Whether the sūtras teach pariṇāma or vivarta is a real textual question with substantive metaphysical consequences. Not a register-effect.

arguments.name: argument-3: jñāna-karma-samuccaya is required by the realist reading
premises: If the world is real (pariṇāma-product of Brahman), then karmic action in the world is also real and salvifically meaningful.
Vedic karma is enjoined by śruti and is binding on the qualified.
Jñāna of Brahman does not abolish karmic obligation, since the world karma operates in is not abolished.
Therefore the path to mokṣa requires the joint cultivation of jñāna and karma — jñāna-karma-samuccaya.
The Advaitin's jñāna-only position presupposes that karmic action is, ultimately, mithyā and so dispensable; this presupposition is false on the realist reading.
arguments.inference: Jñāna-karma-samuccaya is the soteriologically correct path on a realist Vedānta.
arguments.conclusion: The Advaitin's jñāna-only path is a soteriological deviation that follows from the underlying mithyā-vāda error.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi explicit defense of jñāna-only against samuccaya is the principal direct interlocutor here.
Śaṅkara's commentary at BSB 3.4.1 and on Bṛhadāraṇyaka explicitly denies samuccaya.

positions_left_intact.position: Maṇḍana's position is closer to Bhāskara's on this axis (jñāna with karma-and-bhāvanā coordinate); Bhāskara's argument and Maṇḍana's are largely aligned, so the polemic does not engage Maṇḍana adversarially.

later_responses.response_text: Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi 1.55–56 — argues that karma and jñāna stand as bādhya-bādhaka and cannot occupy one locus; this is a structural reply to any samuccaya-position, including Bhāskara's
later_responses.verdict: the reply pre-dates Bhāskara's polemic in its developed form but addresses its core: even on a realist reading, the cognitive structure of action (agent-object) is incompatible with the cognitive structure of brahma-jñāna (non-distinction). The dispute is about whether the samuccaya is structurally possible, not merely about whether it is enjoined.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive on a real soteriological question. The disagreement is not register-dependent: it is a real question whether the cognitive structure of action is compatible with the cognitive structure of brahma-jñāna.

user_thesis_summary: Bhāskara's pracchanna-bauddha polemic is partly substantive and partly register-dependent. The structural-equivalence argument (mithyā ≅ śūnya) is sharpest on a reading of mithyā as asat-equivalent, which the user's framework rejects; on the user's reading, the argument retains some force (against the world's having own-being in any robust sense) but does not establish full equivalence. The vivarta-vs-pariṇāma exegetical argument is genuinely substantive: it raises a real question about how the sūtras' productive-causation language should be read, and the disagreement persists across the post-Bhāskara tradition. The samuccaya argument engages a real soteriological question and pre-supposes a structural disagreement that survives any register-clarification. Importantly, Bhāskara's polemic targets Śaṅkara himself more directly than Rāmānuja's saptavidhānupapatti does: Bhāskara is reading Śaṅkara's bhāṣya at first hand and engaging it as the position to refute. Verdict: substantive on the exegetical and soteriological axes; partially register-dependent on the structural-equivalence axis; does not dissolve under register-clarification at any point. Of the polemic chains in this set, this one most clearly engages Śaṅkara as Śaṅkara rather than the post-Śaṅkara establishment.
primary_source_loci: Bhāskara, Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya, introduction and BSB 1.1.1–4 (the polemical opening against māyā-vāda)
BSB 2.1.14 ff. (defense of pariṇāma-vāda against vivarta)

scholarly_apparatus.work: Fundamentals of Viśiṣṭādvaita: A Comparative Study (Munshiram, 1988), with extensive Bhāskara material
scholarly_apparatus.position: Documents that Bhāskara's polemic against Advaita is the earliest sustained Vedāntic refutation of māyā-vāda, predating Rāmānuja by a generation; the pracchanna-bauddha charge is a structural argument, not a rhetorical insult.

scholarly_apparatus.work: Studien zur Lehre und Entwicklung Śaṅkaras (Vienna, 1979)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Explicitly engages the historical question of whether Śaṅkara's doctrine is influenced by Mādhyamika; the scholarly conclusion is qualified — Gauḍapāda is more directly Mādhyamika-engaged than Śaṅkara himself, but Bhāskara's charge is not without textual basis especially on the Gauḍapāda-Vaitathya material.

scholarly_apparatus.work: Śaṃkara's Arguments against the Buddhists (Philosophy East and West, 1954)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Documents Śaṅkara's own anti-Buddhist polemic at BSB 2.2.18–32 — an important counter to the pracchanna-bauddha charge: Śaṅkara himself explicitly refutes the Buddhist schools and is polemically distant from them.
